name: Acute Pericarditis
body:
**Acute pericarditis** is an inflammation of the pericardium, frequently viral or post-viral in origin. The chest pain is **pleuritic** and retrosternal, **improving when sitting up and leaning forward**, and worsening in the supine position and with deep inspiration. A pericardial friction rub may be present on auscultation. The ECG typically shows **diffuse ST-segment elevation** with **PR-segment depression** in multiple leads (unlike AMI, which exhibits a territorial and reciprocal pattern).

Diagnosis is clinical, supported by ECG, echocardiogram (pericardial effusion), and troponin levels (which may be elevated in cases of myopericarditis). Treatment consists of **NSAIDs** (ibuprofen or ASA at anti-inflammatory doses) and **colchicine** to reduce recurrences, according to guidelines. Avoid anticoagulation if there is a risk of hemopericardium. Hospitalization is indicated in the presence of high fever, significant effusion, immunosuppression, or suspicion of purulent pericarditis.

**Nursing:** maintain a semi-seated position, ensure pain control, monitor for signs of cardiac tamponade (hypotension, jugular venous distension, pulsus paradoxus), and provide patient education regarding relative rest and follow-up.

bibliography.citation: European Society of Cardiology. Pericardial diseases guidelines.
Ministry of Health of Argentina. Care strategies and guidelines.
American College of Cardiology. Pericarditis clinical guidance.
